perturbation.original_text: Confidential information that is disclosed orally will be confirmed in writing by the disclosing party within thirty (30) days after such disclosure. The parties agree that such written confirmation when mailed by the disclosing party, will form a part of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information that is disclosed orally will be confirmed in writing by the disclosing party within ninety (90) days after such disclosure. The parties agree that such written confirmation is optional and does not necessarily form a part of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confirmation period from 30 to 90 days weakens the protection of trade secrets, and making the confirmation optional contradicts standard business practices regarding confidential information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications undermine the definition of what constitutes a trade secret, specifically the efforts made to maintain its secrecy and reasonable steps to protect it. Delaying the written confirmation and making it optional indicates a lack of reasonable measures to protect orally disclosed confidential information, contradicting the UTSA's emphasis on maintaining secrecy. Although there isn't a specific day mentioned in UTSA, the general context is that it is supposed to be timely fashion, not optional.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens trade secret protection by extending the confirmation period from 30 to 90 days and making written confirmation optional. This directly contradicts the UTSA's requirement for 'reasonable measures' to maintain secrecy, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret.' Making confirmation optional and delaying it weakens these measures, suggesting a lack of diligence in protecting confidential information, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise expressly authorized by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to retain the confidential information in confidence for the "Confidential Period" defined in paragraph number 3 below, during which period the receiving party shall not disclose the confidential information to any third party, and shall not use the confidential information for any purpose other than the aforesaid purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise expressly authorized by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to attempt to retain the confidential information in confidence for the "Confidential Period" defined in paragraph number 3 below, during which period the receiving party should minimize disclosing the confidential information to any third party, and shall attempt not to use the confidential information for any purpose other than the aforesaid purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to retain...shall not disclose...shall not use' with 'agrees to attempt to retain...should minimize disclosing...shall attempt not to use' significantly weakens the obligations of the receiving party regarding confidentiality, creating uncertainty and potentially making the agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text aims to protect trade secrets which the Economic Espionage Act also does. By weakening the receiving party's obligations ('agrees' changed to 'agrees to attempt'), the modified text does not adequately ensure the protection of trade secrets, which goes against the intent of the act. It creates ambiguity around what constitutes a violation, impacting legal enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to retain...shall not disclose...shall not use' to 'agrees to attempt to retain...should minimize disclosing...shall attempt not to use' weakens the confidentiality obligations. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 error, Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1832, addresses the 'Theft of trade secrets'. The original text provides a stronger safeguard for trade secrets compared to the changed text. The original text's stronger language aligns more closely with the intent of protecting trade secrets as described in the Law Explanation, while the weakened language in the changed text undermines this protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation and thus creating a contradiction with the purpose of the law.

perturbation.original_text: The “Confidential Period” shall mean two (2) years from the date of receipt of the confidential information or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as confidential information pursuant to paragraph number 5 below.
perturbation.changed_text: The “Confidential Period” shall mean ten (10) years from the date of receipt of the confidential information or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as confidential information pursuant to paragraph number 5 below.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidential period from two years to ten years could be deemed unreasonable or unduly restrictive, especially in industries with rapid technological advancements or high employee turnover. This modification introduces uncertainty about enforceability, as courts may find such an extended period excessively broad.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain trade. While NDAs are generally permissible, an excessively long confidential period, such as ten years, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it prevents former employees from using their general knowledge and skills. By extending the period, the modified text introduces a potential conflict with this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidential period from two to ten years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. This section, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, voids contracts that restrain trade. While NDAs are generally permissible, an excessively long confidential period, such as ten years, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it prevents former employees from using their general knowledge and skills. The modification introduces a potential conflict with this statute, because such a period could prevent someone from engaging in a 'lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind,' which 'is to that extent void,' as per the snippet. The change thus creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each such employee and agent shall be individually bound by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each such employee and agent may be individually bound by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' removes the mandatory obligation for employees and agents to be bound by the agreement, making their adherence to the confidentiality terms optional. This undermines the overall enforceability of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets. By making the NDA optional, there is no legal binding, therefore there is nothing to stop people from disclosing trade secrets. Therefore, it would undermine the act.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may" alters a mandatory obligation to a discretionary one. The Defend Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By making the NDA adherence optional, the changed text undermines the legal protection intended by the Act, as individuals are not legally bound to protect trade secrets, potentially leading to their disclosure without legal recourse.
